title: The Crisis of the European Union: A Response By Jürgen Habermas. Translated by Ciaran Cronin. Polity Press. 2012. 120 pages. $19.95 (cloth)
abstract: This is a small book that nevertheless contains the full spectrum of Jürgen Habermas's political thought. From the German public intellectual criticizing Angela Merkel to the Kantian political philosopher sketching a vision of a cosmopolitan democratic order, the reader gets a comprehensive picture of Habermas's politics. Included in this picture is the characteristic hedging between despair and hope. Habermas the critical-theory pessimist sees very well the deep, almost inescapable pathologies of the contemporary public sphere. But Habermas the heir of Enlightenment optimism is always willing to say, “It's not too late to change course!” Indeed, says the optimist, a proper understanding of the history and function of the EU shows that we are already on that course, but shortsighted elites, led by narrow economic imperatives and followed by a pandering profit-obsessed media, refuse to step up to history and do the right thing. Habermas has long been a champion of the constitutionalization of Europe (the German title of this book is Die Verfassung Europas).The overriding imperative is to bring economic systems within the fold of democratic steering mechanisms. While this mission is a direct application of his social and political theory to real-world politics, Habermas has always been quick to mark a sharp divide between his contributions as lay citizen and his pronouncements as expert philosopher. Habermas, however, is no ordinary citizen but a famous one with access to political elites and media outlets not open to most citizens. Thus, this book, which contains a long essay on the EU and a shorter one on dignity, has been much debated in the media, and has been accompanied by a series of high-profile editorials (two of which are included in the appendix of the book); countless television, radio, and print interviews (one such interview is also included in the appendix); and even an appearance at an SPD policy meeting. The book is policy relevant, as they say, and aims to initiate a public debate that political elites across Europe, but especially in Germany, have been avoiding.
